content:
First, observe that each \(a_i\) lies between 2.56 and 2.86, so any integer approximation \(A_i\) must be either 2 or 3 (if we want \(|A_i-a_i|\) small).  Since we need

\[
\sum_{i=1}^7 A_i \;=\;19,
\]

write \(x\) = the number of 3’s, and \(7-x\) = the number of 2’s.  Then

\[
3x \;+\;2(7-x)\;=\;19
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
x+14 \;=\;19
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
x=5.
\]

So we must choose exactly five of the \(A_i\)’s to be 3 and two of them to be 2.  To minimize the maximum error
\[
M=\max_i|A_i-a_i|,
\]
we should round *down* (to 2) the two smallest \(a_i\) and round *up* (to 3) the remaining five.  The two smallest \(a_i\) are
\[
a_1=2.56,\quad a_2=2.61,
\]
whose errors when rounded to 2 are
\[
|2-2.56|=0.56,\quad |2-2.61|=0.61.
\]
All the others are rounded to 3 with errors
\[
3-2.65=0.35,\;3-2.71=0.29,\;3-2.79=0.21,\;3-2.82=0.18,\;3-2.86=0.14,
\]
none of which exceed 0.61.  One checks easily that if \(M<0.61\), there is no way to get the sum up to 19 with only 2’s and 3’s.  Hence the smallest achievable
\[
M=0.61,
\]
and so
\[
100M=61.
\]
\boxed{61}
